title: The man who paints the lines on the road
summary: Pedro paints the white lines on city roads at night, and he takes pride in work that drivers never notice.
body:
Pedro paints the white lines on the roads. He works at night, when the cars are few. The city is quiet. His machine makes a soft hum. The new paint shines under his light.

People do not think about road lines. A driver sees the white line. The driver follows it. The driver does not think, 'Someone painted this.' That is good, Pedro says. 'If they think about my line, my line is bad. A good line is invisible. You only feel it. You do not see it.'

Pedro has painted lines for twenty-two years. He knows every road in the city. He knows which corner is hard. He knows which line saves lives. There is a sharp bend near the river. Many cars went off the road there before. Pedro painted a bright, wide line. Now the cars stay. The accidents stopped.

'No one will ever thank me for that line,' he says. 'No one knows it is mine. But I drive past with my family on Sunday. I see the line. My son does not know. My wife does not know. But I know. I made that road safe.'

His work disappears slowly. Sun and rain eat the paint. Cars rub it away. So he comes back. He paints it again. The line is never finished. It is a job with no end. Pedro likes that. 'A wall is finished one day,' he says. 'A road is alive. It always needs me.'

annotations.meaningVi: không có điểm dừng / vô tận

grammarNotes.title: Relative clauses with 'which' and 'who'
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Dùng 'which' (cho vật) và 'who' (cho người) để nối thông tin về một danh từ. Trong bài: 'He knows which corner is hard. He knows which line saves lives.' và 'Someone painted this.' Ở A1, hãy chú ý 'which' đứng trước một mệnh đề nói thêm về danh từ phía trước.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Use 'which' (for things) and 'who' (for people) to add information about a noun. From the passage: 'He knows which corner is hard. He knows which line saves lives.' At A1, notice that 'which' introduces a clause telling us more about the noun before it.

translationVi:
Pedro kẻ những vạch trắng trên đường. Anh làm việc ban đêm, khi xe cộ thưa thớt. Thành phố tĩnh lặng. Máy của anh kêu rì rì khe khẽ. Lớp sơn mới lấp lánh dưới ánh đèn của anh.

Người ta không nghĩ về vạch kẻ đường. Người lái xe thấy vạch trắng. Họ đi theo nó. Họ không nghĩ: 'Ai đó đã kẻ cái này.' Vậy là tốt, Pedro nói. 'Nếu họ để ý đến vạch của tôi, thì vạch của tôi tệ. Một vạch tốt là vạch vô hình. Bạn chỉ cảm thấy nó. Bạn không nhìn thấy nó.'

Pedro kẻ vạch được hai mươi hai năm. Anh thuộc mọi con đường trong thành phố. Anh biết khúc nào khó. Anh biết vạch nào cứu mạng người. Có một khúc cua gắt gần sông. Trước đây nhiều xe lao ra khỏi đường ở đó. Pedro kẻ một vạch sáng và rộng. Giờ xe cộ đi đúng làn. Tai nạn dừng hẳn.

'Sẽ chẳng ai cảm ơn tôi vì vạch đó,' anh nói. 'Chẳng ai biết nó là của tôi. Nhưng Chủ nhật tôi lái xe chở gia đình qua đó. Tôi thấy vạch kẻ. Con trai tôi không biết. Vợ tôi không biết. Nhưng tôi biết. Tôi đã làm con đường đó an toàn.'

Việc của anh từ từ biến mất. Nắng mưa ăn mòn lớp sơn. Xe cộ mài nó đi. Nên anh quay lại. Anh kẻ lại lần nữa. Vạch kẻ chẳng bao giờ xong. Đó là công việc không có điểm dừng. Pedro thích thế. 'Một bức tường thì có ngày làm xong,' anh nói. 'Một con đường thì sống. Nó luôn cần tôi.'

options: He paints the white lines on roads
questions.explanation: 'Pedro paints the white lines on the roads.'

questions.prompt: Why does he work at night?
options: Because the cars are few then
Because he sleeps in the day
questions.explanation: 'He works at night, when the cars are few.'

questions.prompt: What does Pedro say makes a good line?
options: It is invisible — you feel it but do not see it
It is very bright and noticed
questions.explanation: 'A good line is invisible. You only feel it. You do not see it.'

questions.prompt: How long has Pedro painted lines?
questions.explanation: 'Pedro has painted lines for twenty-two years.'

questions.prompt: What happened at the sharp bend near the river?
options: After his bright wide line, the accidents stopped
questions.explanation: 'Pedro painted a bright, wide line. Now the cars stay. The accidents stopped.'

questions.prompt: What does Pedro feel when he drives past that line on Sunday?
options: Quiet private pride — he knows it is his, though his family does not
Anger that no one thanks him
questions.explanation: 'My son does not know. My wife does not know. But I know. I made that road safe.'

questions.prompt: Why does Pedro have to repaint the lines?
options: Sun, rain, and cars slowly wear the paint away
He paints them the wrong colour
questions.explanation: 'Sun and rain eat the paint. Cars rub it away.'

questions.prompt: What does Pedro mean when he says 'A road is alive'?
options: The road keeps wearing down, so it always needs his work
questions.explanation: 'A road is alive. It always needs me.'

questions.prompt: Why does Pedro like that his job has no end?
options: Because the road always needs him, unlike a finished wall
questions.explanation: 'A wall is finished one day. A road is alive. It always needs me.'

options: Invisible work can hold deep pride and real worth.
Road lines are a waste of money.
Night work is bad for health.
questions.explanation: Pedro values work no one sees but that saves lives.
questions.hint: Why he is proud of the unseen line.

blanks.explanation: A sharp bend near the river.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Pedro takes deep pride in a line no one knows is his. Is there something you do well that no one notices or credits — and does the lack of thanks make it less valuable to you, or more your own?
